human:
POINTS:
- [build.gradle:802] @gharris1727  without this ignoreFailures extension, check fails for almost any module I see.
v5:
SUMMARY:
This PR introduces the de.thetaphi.forbiddenapis Gradle plugin to detect usage of forbidden JDK APIs (starting with the jdk-unsafe bundled signature). The check is added to all subprojects with ignoreFailures=true for now. The initial approach is reasonable, but there is a significant coverage gap for the :core project.

POINTS:
- [build.gradle:~800] **The `:core` project will be silently excluded from forbidden API checks.** The `:core` project configures `sourceSets { main { java { srcDirs = [] } } }` (lines 1334-1352), routing all sources through the Scala compiler. The forbiddenapis plugin resolves sources from the Java source set, so `forbiddenApisMain` will have ZERO sources to analyze for `:core` — despite 19 Java files existing in `core/src/main/java/`. This creates a coverage gap. Consider configuring the `:core` project explicitly to include its Java sources for forbiddenapis, or document this limitation.

- [build.gradle:800] **`ignoreFailures = true` makes the check effectively non-enforcing.** The check will report violations but never fail the build. This is fine for a discovery phase, but consider adding a follow-up ticket/JIRA to enable enforcement once known violations are addressed, or document this explicitly in the PR description.

- [build.gradle:803] **Test sources are not checked.** The dependency adds only `forbiddenApisMain` to the test task, but the existing checkstyle dependency includes both `checkstyleMain` and `checkstyleTest`. For consistency with how checkstyle is integrated, consider also adding `forbiddenApisTest` to catch forbidden API usage in test code.

- [build.gradle:797-802] **The plugin is applied to ALL subprojects unconditionally.** This includes organizational-only projects like `:connect` (which is just a directory with no code) and `:generator`. While not harmful, consider adding a condition to skip subprojects that have no Java sources, or document that the plugin is applied broadly.

- [build.gradle:800] **Only `jdk-unsafe` bundled signature is included.** This is a reasonable starting point per the PR description ("Part of signatures"). Consider planning to add more signatures later (e.g., `jdk-system-out`, `jdk-non-portable`, `commons-io-unsafe`) to broaden coverage incrementally.
v6:
SUMMARY:
This PR introduces the `de.thetaphi.forbiddenapis` Gradle plugin to detect usage of unsafe JDK APIs (via the `jdk-unsafe` bundled signature) across all subprojects, with failures ignored for now. It's a reasonable first step toward hardening the codebase against problematic API usage.

POINTS:
- [build.gradle:802] **`forbiddenApisTest` is not included in the dependency chain.** The PR adds `forbiddenApisMain` to `test.dependsOn(...)`, but does not add the corresponding `forbiddenApisTest` task. This means forbidden API usage in test sources will not be flagged. If this is intentional (main sources only), document the rationale; otherwise, add `forbiddenApisTest` alongside `forbiddenApisMain` for consistent coverage.

- [build.gradle:800] **`ignoreFailures = true` will never produce actionable output in CI.** With this flag, violations are printed but the build always passes. Since the PR description says "ignoring failures for now," consider whether the plugin should be gated behind a build property (e.g., `project.hasProperty('enforceForbiddenApis')`) so maintainers can enable enforcement during a cleanup sprint without committing to a blocking check immediately.

- [build.gradle:801] **`bundledSignatures` uses a single-signature list; the `jdk-unsafe` signature is a good start but consider `jdk-system-out` and `jdk-deprecated` too.** The `jdk-unsafe` signature covers `Locale.ROOT` vs `Locale.ENGLISH`, `Charset.forName`, and `TimeZone` pitfalls (as noted in the PR description). The [forbidden-apis wiki](https://github.com/policeman-tools/forbidden-apis/wiki/BundledSignatures) offers several other useful bundled signatures that Kafka might want to adopt incrementally.

- [build.gradle:3859] **Missing trailing newline at end of file.** The diff shows the final `}` line has no newline (`\ No newline at end of file`). Most project style guides (and `git diff` conventions) expect a trailing newline.

- [build.gradle:797-802] **No suppression mechanism is configured.** The `forbiddenapis` plugin supports suppression files (via `signaturesFiles`) and inline `@SuppressWarnings("forbiddenapis")` annotations. Since the PR description mentions "Part of signatures" implying this will grow, consider adding an empty suppression file (e.g., under `gradle/`) now so the team can manage false positives organically as more signatures are added, rather than discovering the pattern after violations accumulate.
